title: The Invisible Market
desc: A bazaar where the merchants are invisible. Shopping by touch, smell, and faith.

steps.text:
The market is here. Probably. Your scanner says there are forty-seven creatures within a hundred paces. You can see exactly none of them.

This is Mipedim Oasis's weekly bazaar, and every merchant uses invisibility to display their wares. The theory is that only serious buyers will bother to find what they're looking for. The practice is that you keep bumping into things.

Something brushes your hand. A voice hisses: "Dried sand-scorpion, three credits a pound. Stop stepping on my carpet."

You can't see the carpet. You can't see the sand-scorpion. You're fairly certain you're standing on something that crunches.

options.label: Navigate by smell — find the spice merchant
Navigate by sound — follow the loudest haggling

steps.text:
The spice merchant is easy to find by nose — a warm cloud of cinnamon, pepper, and something that burns your sinuses from thirty feet. You walk toward it, hands outstretched, until your fingers close around a wooden stall that materializes under your grip.

The merchant flickers visible for half a second — long enough to show you three jars of spice before vanishing again. "Red jar is fire-pepper. Blue jar is desert sage. Don't open the green jar."

"What's in the green jar?"

"Don't. Open. The green jar."

You buy fire-pepper and desert sage by feel, counting coins into an invisible palm. The merchant's hand briefly materializes to check the count, then vanishes.

As you leave, curiosity pulls at you. You glance back at where the stall was. The green jar sits alone on the counter, visible, glowing faintly. It wasn't glowing before.

You keep walking.

The loudest haggling comes from somewhere to your left — two invisible voices arguing about the price of a scale-polishing kit with the intensity of a border dispute.

You follow the sound. The argument leads you through a maze of invisible stalls — you know they're there because your shins keep finding them. By the time you reach the hagglers, you've accidentally purchased a clay jar of something that vibrates and been handed a "free sample" of dried fruit by a hand that appeared from nowhere.

The hagglers are two Mipedians, both fully invisible, negotiating by voice alone. The scale-polishing kit sits on a visible mat between them — the only tangible proof that commerce is occurring.

"Forty credits." "Twenty." "Thirty-five." "Twenty-two." "Thirty." "Twenty-five and I tell you where the gem merchant is hiding."

"Deal."

The kit vanishes. Credits clink somewhere. The market continues. You eat the free sample. It's excellent.

You leave the bazaar with lighter pockets, a vibrating jar, and the certainty that you overpaid for everything.
